mixed.question: In Thailand, what is it technically illegal to do to currency bearing the king's image?
options: Keep it in your back pocket
mixed.fact: Stepping on Thai currency is considered an insult to the monarchy and is punishable by law.

mixed.question: In which country is it illegal to run out of fuel on the autobahn?
mixed.fact: German law considers running out of fuel a preventable offense since stopping on the autobahn endangers lives.

mixed.question: In Capri, Italy, what type of footwear was banned for being too noisy in 2025, with fines up to €500?
mixed.fact: Capri banned excessively noisy wooden-soled shoes to preserve tranquility in its narrow historic streets.

mixed.question: In Singapore, which everyday item has been banned from import and sale since 1992, except by prescription?
mixed.fact: The ban was prompted by vandals sticking gum on MRT train door sensors, causing costly disruptions.

mixed.question: What is the approximate temperature of the cosmic microwave background radiation left over from the Big Bang?
mixed.fact: At 2.7K (−270.45°C), this faint glow is the oldest observable light in the universe.

mixed.question: Which moon in our solar system has geysers that shoot ice particles hundreds of kilometers into space, feeding one of Saturn's rings?
mixed.fact: Enceladus's ice geysers supply material to Saturn's diffuse E ring from its subsurface ocean.

mixed.question: True or False: A neutron star is so dense that a teaspoon of its material would weigh approximately 6 billion tons on Earth.
mixed.fact: Neutron stars pack more mass than our Sun into a sphere roughly the size of a city.

mixed.question: Who holds the record for the most Olympic gold medals won by a single athlete?
mixed.fact: Michael Phelps won 23 Olympic gold medals across five Games — eight of them at Beijing 2008 alone.

mixed.question: As of 2025, what is the longest continuously inhabited city in the world, with evidence of settlement spanning over 11,000 years?
mixed.fact: Damascus has archaeological evidence of habitation dating back to approximately 9000 BC.

mixed.question: In 2025, which country broke the world record for the largest share of electricity generated from renewable sources in a single year, exceeding 99%?
mixed.fact: Paraguay generates virtually all its electricity from hydropower, primarily from the massive Itaipú Dam.

science.question: What counterintuitive phenomenon causes a tennis ball served at high speed to dip downward faster than gravity alone would predict?
science.fact: The Magnus effect makes spinning balls curve — topspin creates low pressure above, pulling the ball downward faster.

science.question: True or False: If you could somehow remove all the empty space inside atoms, the entire human race would fit inside a sugar cube.
options: Only half the human race would fit
It would require a basketball-sized volume
science.fact: Atoms are 99.9999999% empty space — compressed atomic nuclei are extraordinarily dense, like neutron star matter.

science.question: In 2025, physicists at CERN reported preliminary evidence for an unexpected anomaly in the behavior of which particle?
science.fact: Refined W boson mass measurements continue to deviate from Standard Model predictions, hinting at unknown physics.

science.question: Which fundamental force is responsible for holding quarks together inside protons and neutrons via gluon exchange?
science.fact: Gluons themselves carry color charge, meaning the strong force uniquely gets stronger as quarks move apart.

science.question: Which deep-sea fish uses a bioluminescent lure but is also one of the few fish species where the male permanently fuses to the female's body?
science.fact: Male anglerfish fuse circulatory systems with females, essentially becoming parasitic sperm-producing organs permanently attached.

science.question: How do mantis shrimp produce their devastating punch that can accelerate faster than a .22 caliber bullet?
options: Hydraulic pressure from body cavity fluid
A spring-loaded saddle-shaped structure in their arm
Explosive chemical reaction in their claws
science.fact: Their saddle structure stores elastic energy like a compressed spring, releasing it independently of muscle speed.

science.question: Which species of sea slug is known for stealing chloroplasts from algae it eats, allowing it to photosynthesize like a plant?
science.fact: Elysia chlorotica can survive months on sunlight alone after incorporating stolen chloroplasts into its own cells.

science.question: What is the recently popularized term for the brain's waste-clearance system that is most active during deep sleep?
science.fact: The glymphatic system flushes toxic proteins like amyloid-beta during sleep — poor sleep may accelerate Alzheimer's.

science.question: Which brain region, once thought to only coordinate movement, is now known to play a major role in emotion, cognition, and language?
science.fact: The cerebellum contains roughly 80% of all neurons in the brain despite being only 10% of its volume.

science.question: True or False: Neurons in the adult human brain can regenerate significantly in the hippocampus throughout life, a process called adult neurogenesis.
options: True — it is well established and universal
False — no new neurons form after age 13
True — but only in the hippocampus and olfactory bulb
False — neurogenesis only occurs in non-human primates
science.fact: Adult neurogenesis is largely restricted to the hippocampus and olfactory bulb, and remains scientifically debated.

history.question: During the Cold War, which country secretly built a nuclear reactor under a football stadium as part of the Manhattan Project's legacy?
history.fact: Chicago Pile-1, the first nuclear reactor, operated beneath the University of Chicago's abandoned football stands in 1942.

history.question: What was the code name for the CIA's covert program that dosed unwitting American citizens with LSD to explore mind control?
history.fact: MKUltra ran from 1953 to 1973 and even tested LSD on CIA employees without their knowledge.

history.question: True or False: During the Cold War, the Soviet Union successfully jammed the Voice of America using more electricity than the broadcasts themselves consumed.
options: Only during the Cuban Missile Crisis
history.fact: Soviet jamming stations used enormous power — sometimes triple the wattage of the Western broadcasts they targeted.

history.question: In 2025, newly declassified documents revealed the CIA considered using what unexpected method to destabilize Castro's public image?
options: Replacing his speeches with parodies on radio
Spraying his shoes with thallium to make his beard fall out
history.fact: The CIA devised bizarre plots against Castro, including thallium-laced shoes to destroy his iconic beard.

history.question: Which ancient Chinese dynasty first established the imperial examination system to select government officials?
history.fact: The Sui Dynasty created imperial exams around 605 AD, but the Tang Dynasty massively expanded them.

history.question: What surprising substance did ancient Chinese alchemists accidentally invent while searching for an elixir of immortality?
history.fact: Taoist alchemists mixing sulfur, charcoal, and saltpeter seeking eternal life ironically created a deadly weapon.

history.question: Which ancient Chinese text, dating to around 200 BCE, contains the earliest known reference to a magnetic compass-like device called a 'south-pointer'?
options: Book of the Devil Valley Master
history.fact: The 'south-pointing spoon' described in this text used lodestone on a bronze plate — predating navigational compasses by centuries.

history.question: Which animal was the first to orbit the Moon and return safely to Earth during the Space Race?
history.fact: In 1968, two tortoises aboard the Soviet Zond 5 became the first Earthlings to fly around the Moon and survive.

history.question: How many Saturn V rockets were launched in total during the Apollo program?
history.fact: All 13 Saturn V launches were successful — an astonishing reliability record for the most powerful rocket ever flown.

history.question: Who was the first person to perform a spacewalk, stepping outside their spacecraft in 1965?
history.fact: Leonov's spacesuit inflated so much in the vacuum that he had to dangerously vent air to fit back inside.

popculture.question: Which 2014 viral meme originated from a scientific debate about color perception and divided the entire internet?
popculture.fact: Neuroscientists confirmed people literally see different colors based on their brain's assumptions about lighting.

popculture.question: What was the original name of the 'Distracted Boyfriend' meme stock photo in its photographer's catalog?
options: Disloyal Man Walking With His Girlfriend
popculture.fact: Photographer Antonio Guillem shot the entire series telling a full story of the couple's relationship.

popculture.question: In 2025, which AI-generated meme format went massively viral, featuring celebrities in unexpected historical painting styles?
options: Renaissance selfies via ChatGPT image generation
popculture.fact: The trend crashed servers multiple times as millions generated portraits simultaneously in early 2025.

popculture.question: True or False: The viral 'Charlie Bit My Finger' video was sold as an NFT for over $760,000 and then removed from YouTube.
options: False — it was never sold as an NFT
popculture.fact: After selling for $760,999 in 2021, the family re-uploaded it to YouTube a few months later.

popculture.question: Which film holds the record for the longest gap between an actor's Oscar nominations for the same franchise?
popculture.fact: Stallone was nominated for Rocky in 1977 and again for Creed in 2016 — a 39-year gap.

popculture.question: Who is the youngest person ever to receive an Academy Award nomination for Best Director?
popculture.fact: John Singleton was just 24 when nominated for Boyz n the Hood in 1992 — a record still unbroken.

popculture.question: How many times has a horror film won the Academy Award for Best Picture?
popculture.fact: Silence of the Lambs (1991) remains the only pure horror film to win Best Picture.

popculture.question: Which 2000s social network required users to rank their friends in a visible 'Top 8' on their profile?
popculture.fact: The Top 8 feature caused so much real-life drama that MySpace eventually let users hide it.

popculture.question: What was the best-selling ringtone of the 2000s in the United States, earning over $10 million?
popculture.fact: Crazy Frog outsold actual music singles in multiple countries, infuriating the music industry.

popculture.question: Which iconic 2000s device was originally codenamed 'Purple' during its secret development at Apple?
popculture.fact: The iPhone team worked in a locked building nicknamed 'The Purple Dorm' due to their grueling hours.

geography.question: Which desert holds the record for the longest period without any recorded rainfall — nearly 400 years?
geography.fact: Parts of the Atacama received zero rainfall from 1570 to 1971 — over 400 years of total drought.

geography.question: True or False: Antarctica is technically classified as a desert.
options: Only the interior is classified as desert
geography.fact: Antarctica receives less than 51mm of precipitation annually, making it the world's largest desert.

geography.question: In 2025, satellite data revealed that which expanding desert had its growth temporarily reversed by unusual monsoon rains?
geography.fact: Unprecedented 2025 monsoon rains caused temporary greening along the Sahara's southern Sahel boundary.

geography.question: The Lut Desert (Dasht-e Lut) in Iran recorded the highest-ever land surface temperature by satellite. How hot?
geography.fact: NASA's Landsat satellite measured 70.7°C on the Lut Desert's dark volcanic surface in 2005.

geography.question: Which Asian country contains more than half of the world's 7,000+ islands classified as 'unnamed'?
geography.fact: Indonesia has over 17,000 islands, and a 2023 recount found thousands still lacked official names.

geography.question: The Caspian Sea borders five countries. Which of these is NOT one of them?
geography.fact: Uzbekistan is doubly landlocked and nowhere near the Caspian, despite being in Central Asia.

geography.question: What is unusual about the Tonlé Sap River in Cambodia?
options: It is the saltiest river in Asia
geography.fact: Mekong flood pressure forces the Tonlé Sap to reverse flow, expanding its lake fivefold during monsoons.

geography.question: Which two South American countries are the only ones that do NOT share a border with Brazil?
geography.fact: Brazil borders every South American country except Chile and Ecuador — that's 10 out of 12 neighbors.

geography.question: How many countries share a land border with China?
geography.fact: China ties with Russia for the most land borders of any country — 14 different neighboring nations.

geography.question: Which tiny European country is bordered entirely by just France and Spain?
geography.fact: Andorra has no airport, no railway, and survived for centuries as a co-principality of France and Spain.

sports.question: Which swimmer became the first person to break the 2-minute barrier in the 200m freestyle, a feat once deemed impossible?
sports.fact: Don Schollander broke 2 minutes in 1963, years before most thought it was achievable.

sports.question: In boxing, who holds the record for the shortest world heavyweight title fight in history?
sports.fact: Tyson knocked out Michael Spinks in just 91 seconds in 1988 for the shortest heavyweight title bout.

sports.question: True or False: An NBA player once recorded a quadruple-double (double digits in four statistical categories) in a single game.
options: True — it has happened four times
True — it has happened exactly twice
sports.fact: Hakeem Olajuwon, Alvin Robertson, Nate Thurmond, and Victor Wembanyama all achieved official quadruple-doubles.

sports.question: Which boxer famously fought with one arm effectively paralyzed for most of a 15-round bout and still nearly won the world title?
sports.fact: Gatti broke his hand in round two against Gabriel Ruelas but fought 10 more rounds with devastating heart.

sports.question: What surprising record did Australian swimmer Ariarne Titmus set at the 2025 World Aquatics Championships?
options: Five individual golds at a single Worlds
First to hold 200m and 800m freestyle records simultaneously
sports.fact: Titmus continued rewriting the record books by smashing her own 400m freestyle world record in 2025.

sports.question: Who was the first athlete in modern Olympics history to win gold medals in both the Summer and Winter Games?
sports.fact: Eddie Eagan won boxing gold in 1920 and bobsled gold in 1932 — still the only person in individual events.

sports.question: Which boxer's professional record of 50-0 remained unbeaten for over 60 years before Floyd Mayweather matched it?
sports.fact: Rocky Marciano retired 49-0 in 1956; Mayweather actually surpassed it by finishing 50-0 in 2017.

sports.question: How many goals did Erling Haaland score in his record-breaking 2024-25 Premier League season?
sports.fact: Haaland shattered the single-season Premier League scoring record, surpassing his own previous mark of 36.

sports.question: In competitive swimming, what counterintuitive rule allows the backstroke start to be the only event where swimmers begin in the water?
options: Swimmers must face the wall at the start
The dive angle is too dangerous on the back
Backstroke was originally a resting stroke
FINA banned out-of-water backstroke starts after 1936 injuries
sports.fact: Backstrokers start in the water because they must face the wall — diving backward off blocks was deemed unsafe.

sports.question: Which sport saw its world record broken and then broken again within the same competition day for the first time at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics?
sports.fact: Bob Beamon's 8.90m long jump shattered the record by 55cm — it stood for 23 years afterward.

food.question: What traditional British dish was originally created as a way to use up leftover vegetables and meat from a Sunday roast?
food.fact: The name comes from the bubbling and squeaking sounds the cabbage makes while frying in the pan.

food.question: Which British savoury spread, made from yeast extract, was originally a byproduct of beer brewing?
food.fact: Marmite was first produced in 1902 in Burton upon Trent, a major centre of British brewing.

food.question: In 2025, which classic British baked good received EU Protected Geographical Indication status after years of campaigning?
food.fact: Yorkshire Parkin's protected status means only versions made in Yorkshire using oatmeal and treacle qualify.

food.question: True or False: The original recipe for Worcestershire sauce was considered a failure and left in a basement for two years before being tasted again.
options: True — it fermented and improved with age
False — it was an instant commercial hit
True — but it was only left for six months
False — it was developed over a single weekend
food.fact: Lea & Perrins abandoned the pungent barrels in 1835, only rediscovering the mellowed sauce years later.

food.question: Which fast food chain was the first to introduce a drive-through window in 1948?
food.fact: In-N-Out Burger pioneered the drive-through using a two-way speaker box at their Baldwin Park location.

food.question: How many hamburgers did White Castle sell in its first year of operation in 1921?
food.fact: White Castle sold nickel burgers to overcome public distrust of ground beef, which was considered low-quality.

food.question: Which fast food item was accidentally invented when a Taco Bell franchisee used a deep fryer to seal a tortilla in the 1980s?
food.fact: The Chalupa's fried flatbread shell was inspired by a traditional Mexican street food of the same name.

food.question: In Sardinia, what is casu marzu — a cheese considered illegal under EU food safety laws but still consumed locally?
options: Cheese soaked in wild boar blood
food.fact: Cheese fly larvae are intentionally introduced; their digestion breaks down fats, creating an ultra-soft texture.

food.question: Which Southeast Asian fruit is banned from many hotels and public transport due to its extremely pungent smell?
food.fact: Durian's smell has been compared to turpentine, gym socks, and sewage — yet fans call it 'King of Fruits.'

food.question: In Iceland, hákarl is a traditional delicacy made from Greenland shark that has been fermented for how long?
food.fact: Fresh Greenland shark flesh is toxic due to high urea content — fermentation makes it safe to eat.

music.question: Which 80s pop icon originally recorded the song 'Tainted Love' that became a massive hit for Soft Cell in 1981?
music.fact: Gloria Jones recorded 'Tainted Love' in 1964 — it flopped initially but became a Northern Soul classic.

music.question: True or False: Cyndi Lauper's 'Girls Just Want to Have Fun' was originally written and recorded by a male artist.
options: It was co-written by Cyndi Lauper
It was originally a jazz standard
music.fact: Robert Hazard wrote and demoed the song in 1979 from a male perspective before Lauper reimagined it.

music.question: What was the only Prince album to feature the Revolution credited on the cover alongside him?
options: Around the World in a Day
music.fact: Despite their Purple Rain fame, 'Around the World in a Day' was the only cover crediting the Revolution.

music.question: In 2025, which 80s pop icon embarked on a widely publicized farewell concert series after decades of retirement from touring?
music.fact: Kate Bush's return to live performance stunned fans, having barely toured since her 1979 Tour of Life.

music.question: Singer-songwriter Elliott Smith was posthumously nominated for an Oscar for which film's soundtrack?
music.fact: Smith performed 'Miss Misery' at the 1998 Oscars, losing to Celine Dion's 'My Heart Will Go On.'

music.question: Which singer-songwriter famously wrote 'Hallelujah,' a song that took five years and roughly 80 draft verses to complete?
music.fact: Cohen wrote about 80 verses, sometimes working on lyrics in his underwear on his hotel room floor.

music.question: How many instruments did singer-songwriter Sufjan Stevens play on his album 'Illinois'?
music.fact: Stevens played banjo, piano, guitar, drums, recorder, oboe, and many more — nearly all instruments himself.

music.question: Which jazz legend was known for playing trumpet with his cheeks puffed out enormously, a technique doctors warned could be dangerous?
music.fact: Gillespie's ballooned cheeks (laryngocele) became his trademark, caused by decades of extreme air pressure.

music.question: What unusual instrument did blues pioneer Robert Johnson reportedly tune to open D to record 'Cross Road Blues'?
music.fact: Johnson likely used a cheap Kalamazoo KG-14 for many recordings — worth thousands today as collectibles.

music.question: Which jazz pianist composed 'Round Midnight,' the most recorded jazz standard by a jazz composer?
music.fact: Monk wrote ''Round Midnight' at just 17 years old — it has since been recorded over 1,000 times.

technology.question: Which programming language was originally called 'Oak' before being renamed due to a trademark conflict?
technology.fact: Java was renamed after the developers' favorite coffee, sourced from Java island in Indonesia.

technology.question: In Rust, what term describes the compile-time system that tracks ownership, borrowing, and lifetimes of references?
technology.fact: Rust's borrow checker eliminates data races at compile time — no runtime garbage collection needed.

technology.question: Which programming language introduced 'pattern matching for switch' (JEP 441) as a finalized feature in 2023, reshaping how developers write conditional logic?
technology.fact: Java 21's pattern matching lets switch statements deconstruct records and check types simultaneously.

technology.question: What is the name of the esoteric programming language where the only valid characters are the eight punctuation marks: + - < > [ ] . , ?
technology.fact: Brainfuck operates on a 30,000-cell memory array and is Turing-complete despite having only 8 commands.

technology.question: True or False: The 2024 CrowdStrike outage that crashed millions of Windows machines worldwide was caused by a cyberattack.
options: True — it was a sophisticated supply-chain attack
True — it was ransomware targeting kernel drivers
False — it was caused by a faulty content update to their Falcon sensor
False — it was a Microsoft Windows kernel bug unrelated to CrowdStrike
technology.fact: A single defective channel file update caused 8.5 million Windows devices to blue-screen simultaneously.

technology.question: What is the name of the attack technique where hackers intercept and alter communication between two parties without either knowing?
technology.fact: HTTPS and certificate pinning were specifically designed to prevent man-in-the-middle interception.

technology.question: Which notorious hacking group, linked to North Korea, stole $1.5 billion in cryptocurrency from the Bybit exchange in early 2025?
technology.fact: The Lazarus Group's Bybit heist was the largest single cryptocurrency theft in history.

technology.question: What does the 'T' stand for in the AI architecture name 'GPT' used by OpenAI's language models?
technology.fact: The Transformer architecture was invented at Google in 2017 but became famous through OpenAI's GPT models.

technology.question: Which machine learning technique trains a model by having two neural networks — a generator and a discriminator — compete against each other?
technology.fact: Ian Goodfellow conceived GANs during a bar argument in 2014 and coded the first version that same night.

technology.question: In the AI concept of 'catastrophic forgetting,' what surprising problem occurs when a neural network learns new tasks?
options: It runs out of memory and crashes
It dramatically forgets previously learned tasks
It duplicates weights across all layers
technology.fact: Unlike human brains, standard neural networks can completely overwrite old knowledge when trained on new data.

nature.question: Which fungus can control the behavior of ants, forcing them to climb to a specific height before killing them?
nature.fact: The fungus manipulates ant muscles directly — the ant's brain remains largely uninfected during the 'zombie' phase.

nature.question: True or False: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon's Blue Mountains spanning over 2,300 acres.
options: True, but it's in Washington state
True, but it spans only 600 acres
nature.fact: Armillaria ostoyae is estimated to be between 2,400 and 8,650 years old and weighs more than a blue whale.

nature.question: In 2025, researchers discovered that certain soil fungi can break down which notoriously persistent pollutant class?
nature.fact: Some fungi use specialized enzymes to cleave the ultra-strong carbon-fluorine bonds in PFAS compounds.

nature.question: What unusual survival strategy does the tardigrade-associated fungus Purpureocillium lilacinum share with tardigrades themselves?
options: It produces antifreeze proteins to survive cryptobiosis-like desiccation
It generates its own magnetic field
nature.fact: This fungus produces trehalose and specialized proteins remarkably similar to tardigrade survival mechanisms.

nature.question: Which massive prehistoric shark had teeth serrated like steak knives and could reach lengths of approximately 50 feet?
nature.fact: Megalodon's bite force is estimated at over 40,000 pounds — roughly ten times stronger than a great white shark.

nature.question: What surprising feature did the Jurassic dinosaur Yi qi possess that is unique among all known dinosaurs?
nature.fact: Yi qi had rod-like bones extending from its wrists supporting membrane wings — completely unlike feathered flight.

nature.question: How many mass extinction events have occurred in Earth's history that wiped out more than 75% of all species?
nature.fact: The 'Big Five' extinctions include the Permian event, which killed roughly 96% of all marine species.

nature.question: Which coral reef species practices 'farming' by cultivating specific algae gardens and aggressively defending them from intruders?
nature.fact: Damselfish weed out unwanted algae species and attack fish up to ten times their size to protect their farms.

nature.question: What percentage of the ocean floor do coral reefs cover, despite supporting roughly 25% of all marine species?
nature.fact: Coral reefs cover just 0.1% of the ocean floor yet harbor more species per square meter than any other marine environment.

nature.question: Which venomous marine snail, found on coral reefs, uses a hollow harpoon-like tooth to inject prey with a cocktail of over 100 different toxins?
nature.fact: Cone snail venom contains compounds now used to develop painkillers 1,000 times more potent than morphine.

film.question: In the original 'Texas Chain Saw Massacre' (1974), what was the chainsaw prop actually powered by?
film.fact: Gunnar Hansen wielded a real, functional chainsaw on set — actors' terror was often genuine.

film.question: Which 2025 horror film directed by Robert Eggers reimagined a classic vampire tale and became a box-office hit?
film.fact: Eggers spent over a decade developing his Nosferatu remake before it finally reached theaters.

film.question: True or False: In 'The Shining' (1980), the blood elevator scene used roughly 200 gallons of fake blood.
options: False — it was closer to 3,000 gallons
True — filmed in a single take
False — it was real chocolate syrup
film.fact: Kubrick used approximately 3,000 gallons of fake blood, and filming that single scene took over a year.

film.question: Who composed the iconic screeching violin score for Alfred Hitchcock's 'Psycho' (1960)?
film.fact: Hitchcock originally wanted the shower scene silent — Herrmann's strings changed cinema history forever.

film.question: In Ridley Scott's 'Alien' (1979), what real organic materials were used to construct the interior of the alien egg?
options: Sheep intestines and cow stomach lining
film.fact: The egg's interior membrane used cattle organs from a local slaughterhouse, creating that disturbingly organic look.

film.question: Which 1997 sci-fi film flopped at the box office but was later reappraised as a sharp political satire?
film.fact: Director Paul Verhoeven intentionally modeled the Federation's propaganda after Nazi-era recruitment films.

film.question: How many different versions of 'Blade Runner' have been officially released as of 2025?
film.fact: The seven cuts include the Workprint, US Theatrical, International, Director's Cut, and the 2007 Final Cut.

film.question: Which Iranian film won the Academy Award for Best International Feature Film in 2012, a first for Iran?
film.fact: Asghar Farhadi's 'A Separation' also won the Golden Bear at Berlin — a rare double triumph.

film.question: In Bong Joon-ho's 'Parasite' (2019), what specific food dish serves as a recurring class-divide symbol?
film.fact: Ram-don is cheap instant noodles topped with expensive sirloin — perfectly embodying the film's class tension.

film.question: Which legendary Japanese director made over 30 films but reportedly destroyed the negatives of several early works himself?
film.fact: Kurosawa burned negatives of works he deemed unworthy, and several early films are now considered permanently lost.

politics.question: Which world leader was caught on a hot mic in 2025 calling a fellow G7 leader 'totally out of his depth'?
politics.fact: Macron has a history of hot-mic incidents, including mocking leaders at previous NATO summits.

politics.question: What was the name of the scandal where Italian politicians were found using public funds to renovate luxury vacation homes in 2006?
politics.fact: The name literally translates to 'clever guys of the little apartment block' — a sardonic Italian nickname.

politics.question: Which U.S. Vice President resigned due to criminal charges of tax evasion and money laundering, completely unrelated to Watergate?
politics.fact: Agnew accepted bribes in envelopes inside the White House itself while serving as Vice President.

politics.question: True or False: South Korean President Roh Moo-hyun was impeached in 2004 simply for verbally endorsing a political party before an election.
politics.fact: The Constitutional Court later overturned it, calling the violation too minor — he returned to office after 63 days.

politics.question: Which Pacific island nation was the last country on Earth to grant women the right to vote, doing so only in 2005?
politics.fact: Kuwait granted women's suffrage in May 2005, but Saudi Arabia only allowed women to vote in municipal elections in 2015.

politics.question: How many countries worldwide currently enforce compulsory voting laws with penalties for non-voters?
politics.fact: Australia fines non-voters roughly $20 — yet turnout still only reaches around 91%, not 100%.

politics.question: Which European country used a citizens' assembly of randomly selected people to draft a new constitution after its 2008 financial crisis?
politics.fact: Iceland's 'crowdsourced constitution' was approved by referendum but was never formally adopted by parliament.

politics.question: During the 1956 Hungarian Revolution, rebels famously cut a symbol out of the center of their national flag. What was it?
politics.fact: The flag with a hole became the revolution's iconic symbol and is now a protected national relic in Hungary.

politics.question: Which 18th-century revolution saw formerly enslaved people defeat Napoleon's army and establish the first Black republic?
politics.fact: Haiti's revolution was so threatening to slaveholding powers that the U.S. didn't recognize Haiti until 1862.

politics.question: Who was the unlikely teenage woman who became a key symbol of Sudan's 2019 revolution, photographed standing atop a car leading protest chants?
politics.fact: Alaa Salah was dubbed 'Kandaka' — the ancient Nubian title for queen — by protesters and global media alike.
